matuww

Writing Scripts Descended from Egyptian Hieroglyphs

× About Glossary Credits
☰


Greek

Ελληνικό

Alphabet1
4 c. BCE - Present1


Right-to-left1

Languages1

Greek

Places1

Greece and Cyprus, and formerly the territories of the Hellenistic World and Eastern Roman Empire

Evolution1-3

Capitals

Capitals are angular letters with straight lines with each letter individually formed distinctly and clearly separated from the others.
They are commonly found in inscriptions on hard materials like stone or metal.
Capitals are majuscule.

Post-Euclidean Archaic Greek Capitals

 403 BCE - 4 c. BCE
Archaic Greek letter Alpha
×

Archaic Greek letter Alpha

a
IPA: /a/, /aː/

Derived from Phoenician letter ʾālep (𐤀).

Numerical value: 1

Archaic Greek letter Beta
×

Archaic Greek letter Beta

b
IPA: /b/

Derived from Phoenician letter Bēt (𐤁).

IPA: /ɡ/

Uncials

Uncials are derived from Capitals. These letters are less angular and have more curves as they are written on papyrus and vellum.
Like Capitals, most letters show bilinear quality - being bound by two horizontal lines that determine letter height and size.
Uncials are majuscule.

Ptolemaic Papyral
Greek Uncials

 323 - 30 BCE

A variant of Biblical Uncials developed into Coptic Uncials, which formed the basis for the Coptic alphabet.

Sloping Uncials developed to become Slavonic Uncials, which formed the basis for the Early Cyrillic alphabet.

Slavonic

Cursives

Cursives are derived from Uncials. Often used in daily writing where letters are often simplified and ligatures are common.
They are often used in conjunction with Uncials.

Early Greek Cursives

 2 c. BCE

Early Greek Cursive letter Beta (variant 1)
Early Greek Cursive letter Gamma (variant 1)

Minuscules

Minuscules are derived from Cursives. These eventually replaced Uncials.

 9 - Mid-10 c. CE

Diacritics

Acute accent
×

Acute accent

ά

Ὀξεῖα

Oxeîa
Indiates a high pitch on a short vowel or a rising pitch on a long vowel.

Can be combined with breathing diacritics.

Grave accent
×

Grave accent

ὰ

Βαρεῖα

Bareîa
Indiates a normal or low pitch.

Can be combined with breathing diacritics.

Circumflex (variant 1)
×

Circumflex (variant 1)

ᾶ

Περισπωμένη

Perispōménē
Indiates a high and falling pitch within one syllable.

Can be combined with breathing diacritics.

Circumflex (variant 2)
×

Circumflex (variant 2)

ᾶ

Περισπωμένη

Perispōménē
Indiates a high and falling pitch within one syllable.

Can be combined with breathing diacritics.

Rough breathing
×

Rough breathing

ἁ

Δασὺ πνεῦμα

Dasù pneûma
Indicates a voiceless glottal fricative (/h/) before the vowel in Ancient Greek.

Can be combined with accent diacritics.

Smooth breathing
×

Smooth breathing

ἁ

Ψιλὸν πνεῦμα

Psīlòn pneûma
Indicates the absence of a voiceless glottal fricative (/h/) before the vowel in Ancient Greek.

Can be combined with accent diacritics.

Coronis
×

Coronis

ἁ

Κορωνίς

Indicates a contracted vowel.

Appears identical to the smooth breathing diacritic.

Iota subscript
×

Iota subscript

Ὑπογεγραμμένη

Hypogegramménē
Used under long vowels ᾱ, η, and ω
to mark the ancient long diphthongs ᾱι, ηι, and ωι,
in which the ι is no longer pronounced.

Iota adscript
×

Iota adscript

Προσγεγραμμένη

Prosgegramménē
Diaeresis
×

Diaeresis

ϊ

Διαίρεσις

Used on ι and υ to show that a pair of vowel letters is pronounced separately,
rather than as a diphthong or as a digraph for a simple vowel.

Ligatures4

Notable ligatures
Greek ligature Tau-Rho
×

Greek ligature Tau-Rho

⳨

In Pre-Christian times, it served as a symbol for the trikhalkon, a copper coin, from its first two letters.

Later standing for σταυρός (stavrós) "cross".

=

Greek majuscule letter Tau

+

Greek majuscule letter Rho
Greek ligature Chi-Rho
×

Greek ligature Chi-Rho

☧

In Pre-Christian times, it stood for ΧΡΗΣΤΟΝ (khrēstón) "good", from its first two letters.

Later standing for ΧΡΙΣΤΟΣ (Christos) "Christ", from its first two letters.

=

Greek majuscule letter Chi

+

Greek majuscule letter Rho
Greek ligature IX
×

Greek ligature IX

Derived from the initials of Ιησους Χριστος (Iesous Christos) "Jesus Christ".

=

Greek majuscule letter Iota

+

Greek majuscule letter Chi

Abbreviations5

Nomina sacra

Ιερές συντομογραφίες

Frequently used abbreviations of divine names or titles, typically denoted by capital letters with an overline.

Greek nomen sacrum Theós
×

Greek nomen sacrum Theós

ΘΣ

ΘΕΟΣ

Theós

Iēsous

ΠΝΕΥΜΑ

Patēr

Epigraphy, Paleography, and Typography

Capitals

Classical Greek Capitals6

Fragment of a marble stele recording the decree that appointed a commission to take charge of the unfinished Erechtheum and to resume building operations. c. 450-403 BCE, Attica, Greece. (description - here)


Hellenistic Greek Capitals7

Marble wall block from the temple of Athena at Priene, inscribed on two sides. The inscription on the front records the gift of funds from Alexander the Great to complete the temple. c. 334-330 BCE, Priene, Ionia. (description - here)


Uncials

Ptolemaic Papyral Greek Uncials8

Bacchylides, Epinicians, c. 2-3 c. CE. Oxyrhynchus, Egypt (description - here)


Roman Papyral Greek Uncials9

P.Hawara 24-28 (Bodleian Libr., Gr. Class. A.1 (P)). Homer, Iliad I. c. 2 c. CE. Fayum, Egypt (description - here)


Byzantine Papyral Greek Uncials10

P.Bodmer XXIV (Rahlfs 2110). c. 225–325 CE. Egypt. (description - here)


Biblical Greek Uncials11

Codex Bezae Cantabrigiensis, Matthew 11:10-21. c. 3 c. CE. Egypt. (description - here)


Coptic Greek Uncials12

Codex Marchalianus, Old Testament. c. 700 CE. Egypt. (description - here)


Sloping Greek Uncials13

Codex Washingtonensis, The Four Gospels. 4-5 CE. Egypt. (description - here)


Slavonic Greek Uncials14

Codex Boreelianus, The Four Gospels. c. 1000-1025 CE. Constantinople, Byzantine Empire. (description - here)


Minuscules

Codices Vetustissimi15

Venetus A. The Iliad. c. 950 CE. Constantinople, Byzantine Empire. (description - here)


Codices Vetusi16

Codex Sinaiticus. St. John the Evangelist with St. Prochorus (miniature) and the beginning of the Gospel of John. c. 13-15 c. CE. Sinai. (description - here)


Codices Recentiores17

Barocci 131. Miscellany of Greek classical and Byzantine texts by Michael Psellos. c. 13 c. CE. Constantiople, Byzantine Empire. (description - here)


Codices Novelli18

Page showing the beginning of the Gospel of John from Robert Estienne's edition of the New Testament, printed in Claude Garamond's typeface Grecs du roi. 1550 CE. Paris, France. (description - here)


First two stanzas of
Hýmnos eis tin Eleftherían
(Hymn to Liberty)
in Greek19-21

Greek

Σὲ γνωρίζω ἀπὸ τὴν κόψι
Τοῦ σπαθιοῦ τὴν τρομερή,
Σὲ γνωρίζω ἀπὸ τὴν ὄψι,
Ποὺ μὲ βία μετράει τὴν γῆ.

Ἀπ' τὰ κόκκαλα βγαλμένη
Τῶν Ἑλλήνων τὰ ἱερά,
Καὶ σὰν πρῶτα ἀνδρειωμένη,
Χαῖρε, ὢ χαῖρε, Ἐλευθεριά!

Transliteration

Se gnorízo apó tin kópsi
Tou spathioú tin tromerí,
Se gnorízo apó tin ópsi,
Pou me viá metráei ti gi.

Ap' ta kókkala vgalméni
Ton Hellínon ta hierá,
Kai san próta andreioméni,
Khaíre, o khaíre, Eleftheriá!

Translation

I know you from the blade
Of the sword, the terrifying [blade],
I know you from the appearance,
Which, with force, measures the earth.

From the bones taken out,
The sacred ones of the Greeks
And, as at first, again emboldened,
Rejoice, oh rejoice, liberty!


Keyboard22

Greek keyboard on Gboard.


Script comparison1,3,23-30

Writing Script Head of ox House plan Throw stick Nile tilapia Man with arms raised Pear shaped mace Two diagonal strokes Plan of rectangular enclosure Heart and trachea Arm with downward facing palm Hand Crook Water ripple Cobra Reed column Eye Mouth Rush with shoots Cord on stick Head Bow Crossed sticks

Logography
            
32 c. BCE - 4 c. CE
Egyptian Hieroglyph Gardiner's Sign No. F1
×

Egyptian Hieroglyph Gardiner's Sign No. F1

𓃾

Head of Ox

Ideogram for: kꜣ “cattle”
Reconstructed Old Egyptian pronunciation: /kaʀ/
Reconstructed Middle Egyptian pronunciation: /kaʀ/
Reconstructed Late Egyptian pronunciation: /kaʔ/
Modern Egyptological pronunciation: /kɑ/
Conventional Anglicisation: ka

Reconstructed Middle Egyptian pronunciation: /ˈpaːɾuw/
Reconstructed Medio-Late Egyptian pronunciation: /ˈpaːɾəʔ/
Reconstructed Late Egyptian pronunciation: /poːɾ/
Modern Egyptological pronunciation: /pɛr/
Conventional Anglicisation: per

Conventional Anglicisation: qema

w

Ecclesiastical Bohairic pronunciation: /n/

x

Coptic letter Chi (majuscule)
×

Coptic letter Chi (majuscule)

Ⲭ

Ⲭⲓ
k/sh/kh
Reconstructed Classical Sahidic pronunciation: /kh/
Reconstructed Classical Bohairic pronunciation: /kʰ/
Traditional Bohairic pronunciation: /k/
Ecclesiastical Bohairic pronunciation: /k/ (if the word is Coptic in origin), /x/ (if the word is Greek in origin), /ç/ (if the word is Greek in origin but before /e̞/ or /i/)

Derived from Coptic Greek Uncial letter Chi (minuscule) (χ).

Numerical value: 600

Lineage1,3,28,29,31-42


Egyptian Hieroglyphs
 ↳Hieratic Egyptian
  ↳Proto-Sinaitic
   ↳Phoenician
    ↳Archaic Greek
     ↳Etruscan
     ↳Lydian
     ↳Carian
     ↳Messapic
     ↳Lycian
     ↳Greco-Iberian
     ↳Classical Greek Capitals
      ↳Hellenistic Greek Capitals
       ↳Bactrian
       ↳Ptolemaic Greek Uncials
        ↳Roman Greek Uncials
         ↳Byzantine Greek Uncials
          ↳Biblical Greek Uncials
           ↳Coptic Greek Uncials
            ↳Coptic
          ↳Sloping Greek Uncials
           ↳Slavonic Greek Uncials
            ↳Early Cyrillic
          ↳Modern Greek

Commission artwork by xeniok‬ (Bluesky page - here)


References


1. Daniels, P. T., & Bright, W. (Eds.). (1996). The world’s writing systems. Oxford University Press.
2. D’Alessandro, G., Ortimini, P., & Papapicco, A. (2022). Mnamon: Greco alfabetico. Laboratorio di Storia, Archeologia, Epigrafia e Tradizione dell’antico (SAET) - Scuola Normale Superiore. https://doi.org/10.25429/SNS.IT/LETTERE/MNAMON029
3. Marcos, J-J. (2024). Fonts for Greek Paleography. Typofonts.com. https://typofonts.com/GREEK_PALEOGRAPHY.pdf
4. Syropoulos, A. (2007). The Philokalia Package. https://web.archive.org/web/20120525190919/http://www.math.washington.edu/tex-archive/fonts/philokalia/philokalia.pdf
5. Metzger, B. M. (1981). Manuscripts of the Greek bible: An introduction to palaeography. Oxford University Press.
6. 693356001. British Museum, London, United Kingdom. 450BCE to 403 BCE. Retrieved July 14, 2025, from https://www.britishmuseum.org/collection/object/G_1816-0610-273
7. 353521001. British Museum, London, United Kingdom. 334 BCE to 330 BCE Retrieved July 14, 2025, from https://www.britishmuseum.org/collection/object/G_1870-0320-88
8. Oxford Papyrology (2022). P.Oxy. XXIII 2363. Bacchylides, Epinicians. University of Oxford. Online resource. https://doi.org/10.25446/oxford.21164029.v1
9. P.Hawara 24-28 (Bodleian Libr., Gr. Class. A.1 (P)). Cairo Museum, Cairo, Egypt. c. 2 c. CE. Retrieved July 15, 2025, from http://ipap.csad.ox.ac.uk/4DLink4/4DACTION/IPAPwebquery?vPub=Pack&vVol=&vNum=616
10. P.Bodmer XXIV (Rahlfs 2110). Museum of the Bible, Washington, DC, United States. c. 225–325 CE. Retrieved July 15, 2025, from https://collections.museumofthebible.org/artifacts/42463-pbodmer-xxiv-rahlfs-2110
11. Codex Bezae Cantabrigiensis. University of Cambridge, Cambrifdge, United Kingdom. c. 3 c. CE. Retrieved July 15, 2025, from https://cudl.lib.cam.ac.uk/view/MS-NN-00002-00041/15

14. Codex Boreelianus. Utrecht University Library, Utrecht, Netherlands. 1000-1025 CE. Retrieved July 16, 2025, from https://objects.library.uu.nl/reader/index.php?obj=1874-25529&lan=en&_ga=2.85309210.2070287935.1592822300-937085898.1579696460#page//65/36/34/65363467298358902882240562630464685698.jpg/mode/1up
15. Venetus A. Biblioteca Sansoviniana, Venice, Italy. c. 950 CE. Retrieved July 16, 2025, from https://www.historyofinformation.com/detail.php?id=1543
16. Codex Sinaiticus. National Library of Russia, Saint Petersburg, Russia. c. 13-15 c. CE. Retrieved July 16, 2025, from https://expositions.nlr.ru/eng/ex_manus/CodexSinaiticus/sobr_p.php
17. Psellos, M. Barocci 131. Bodleian Libraries, University of Oxford, Oxford, United Kingdom, c. 13 c. CE. Retrieved July 16, 2025, from https://digital.bodleian.ox.ac.uk/objects/8f71e031-ea60-436f-937b-81275c271527/surfaces/650edf38-fbad-4534-8d39-09f8b933b51f/
18. Estienne, R. Gospel. 1550 CE. Retrieved July 16, 2025, from https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Gospel_Estienne_1550.jpg
19. Σολωμος, Δ. (1825). Υμνος εις την ελευθεριαν. Historikē kai Ethnologikē Hetaireia tēs Hellados.
20. Duncan, L. (2016, July 30). Poem of the day: Hymn to liberty by Rudyard Kipling. The Herald. https://www.heraldscotland.com/life_style/arts_ents/14652246.poem-day-hymn-liberty-rudyard-kipling/
21. Mavronica E. Recording of live reading of First two stanzas of "Hýmnos eis tin Eleftherían" in Greek. MP3 file. November 31 2025.
22. Google. (2016). Gboard.
23. Colless, Brian E (1990). "The proto-alphabetic inscriptions of Sinai". Abr-Nahrain / Ancient Near Eastern Studies. 28: 1–52. doi:10.2143/anes.28.0.525711
24. Colless, Brian E (1991). "The proto-alphabetic inscriptions of Canaan". Abr-Nahrain / Ancient Near Eastern Studies. 29: 18–66. doi:10.2143/anes.29.0.525718

27. Adiego, I. J. (2006). The Carian Language. Leiden, Netherlands: Brill.
28. Little, C. C. (2011). Proposal to Encode Additional Old Italic Characters. Retrieved July 17, 2025, from unicode.org website: https://www.unicode.org/L2/L2011/11146r-old-italic.pdf
29. Everson, M. (2006). Proposal to encode the Lycian and Lydian scripts in the SMP of the UCS. Retrieved July 17, 2025, from dkuug.dk website: http://std.dkuug.dk/jtc1/sc2/wg2/docs/n3019.pdf

31. Allen, J. P. (2014). Middle Egyptian: An introduction to the language and culture of hieroglyphs. Cambridge, England: Cambridge University Press.
32. Albright, William F. (1966). The Proto-Sinaitic Inscriptions and their Decipherment.
33. Davidson, L. (2022, March 18). How the Phoenician Alphabet Revolutionised Language. Retrieved July 17, 2025, from historyhit.com website: https://www.historyhit.com/how-the-phoenician-alphabet-revolutionised-language/
34. Woodard, R. D. (2013). A companion to the ancient Greek language (E. J. Bakker, Ed.). Wiley-Blackwell.
35. Jeffery, L. H. (1990). The local scripts of archaic Greece: A study of the origin of the Greek alphabet and its development from the eighth to the fifth centuries BC. Clarendon Press.
36. Shaked, Saul (1987). "Aramaic". Encyclopædia Iranica. Vol. 2. New York: Routledge & Kegan Paul. pp. 250–261. p. 251
37. Bakker, E. J. (Ed.). (2010). A companion to the ancient Greek language: Bakker/A companion to the ancient Greek language. Wiley-Blackwell. 
38. Chaker, P. S. (n.d.). Etat des lieux et perspectives. Centrederechercheberbere.Fr. Retrieved July 17, 2025, from https://www.centrederechercheberbere.fr/tl_files/doc-pdf/Libyque_Synthese.pdf
39. Cross, F. M. (2003). Leaves from an epigrapher’s notebook: Collected papers in Hebrew and west Semitic palaeography and epigraphy. doi:10.1163/9789004369887
40. Naso, A. (Ed.). (2017). Etruscology. doi:10.1515/9781934078495
41. Sinner, A. G., & Velaza, J. (Eds.). (2019). Palaeohispanic languages and epigraphies. Oxford University Press.
